abstract:
AI code generators like OpenAI Codex have the potential to assist novice programmers by generating code from natural language descriptions; however, over-reliance on these tools might negatively impact learning and retention. To explore the implications of AI code generators on introductory programming, we conducted a controlled experiment with 69 novices (ages 10-17). Learners worked on 45 Python code-authoring tasks—half with access to Codex and half without—each followed by a manual code-modification task. 

Our results show that using Codex significantly increased code-authoring performance, leading to a 1.15x increase in completion rates and 1.8x higher correctness scores, without decreasing performance on subsequent manual modification tasks. Furthermore, learners who had access to Codex during the training phase performed slightly better on evaluation post-tests conducted one week later, though this difference did not reach statistical significance. Notably, learners with higher baseline programming aptitude (as measured by a Scratch pre-test) performed significantly better on retention post-tests if they had prior access to Codex. These findings suggest that AI assistants can provide effective scaffolding for novices without necessarily creating a detrimental dependency.
